title: Woven placemats
subtitle: A set of four plain-weave cotton placemats on a rigid heddle loom
excerpt: Weave a set of four placemats in plain weave on a rigid heddle loom using 8/2 cotton warp and a contrasting cotton weft. Each mat finishes at approximately 35 x 45 cm after wet-finishing. The plain weave structure means placemats withstand repeated machine washing without distortion.
sourceNotes: Cotton placemats in plain weave are a standard beginner project in rigid heddle instruction and have been a staple of weaving curricula since the mid-20th century.

content.text: Plain weave on a rigid heddle produces a balanced, hardwearing cloth suitable for daily-use kitchen textiles. The key variable is
: for 8/2 cotton on a standard rigid heddle, a 15-dent reed (15 threads per 10 cm) gives a balanced weave. Check your loom's available reed sizes and choose the closest to 15 dent.

items.name: Rigid heddle loom, at least 45 cm weaving width

content.text: Four mats at 45 cm woven width with 15 threads per 10 cm: 15 x 4.5 = 67.5, rounded to 68 warp ends. Each mat weaves 50 cm on the loom (allowing about 25% take-up and shrinkage to finish at 35-40 cm after wet-finishing). Four mats plus loom waste: 4 x 55 cm + 50 cm waste = 270 cm (2.7 m) per warp end. Total warp: 68 ends x 2.7 m = approximately 184 m of warp yarn.

Measure the warp to 2.7 m and thread through the rigid heddle alternately in slots and holes (one thread per slot, one per hole, alternating across the full 45 cm weaving width). Tie onto the front apron rod in small groups of 8-10 ends. Even the tension across the full width before beginning to weave.

Weave a 3-4 cm header of waste yarn to spread the warp evenly, then remove after wet-finishing. Begin the first mat. Alternate the heddle between up-shed and down-shed, passing the shuttle through each shed and beating with a consistent, firm beat after each pass. Weave 50 cm for the first mat, insert two picks of contrast yarn to mark the boundary, weave the next 50 cm for the second mat, and continue. The marks are cut away after wet-finishing.

Cut the mats apart at the marking rows. Machine-wash the mats on a 40-degree cotton cycle to full and shrink them to their finished dimensions. Dry flat and press with a warm iron while slightly damp. The weft edges need no hemming: machine washing will fray the cut ends slightly, but they can be trimmed or left as a fringe. For a finished hem, fold 1 cm to the back before the final press and stitch by machine.

items.fix: Throw the weft in a higher arc across the shed (like a gentle hill) so there is extra weft length to accommodate the over-and-under path before beating. Never pull the weft taut before beating.
items.cause: Weft thrown at too low an arc before beating
items.problem: The mats are narrower in the middle than at the edges (selvedge pull-in)

items.fix: After throwing the shuttle, hold the weft at the entry selvedge with one finger and gently pull the excess back into the shed before beating. The selvedge thread should have just enough weft to turn without a loop.
items.cause: Weft left too slack at the turn at each edge
items.problem: The weave structure shows loose loops at the selvedge
